coaching.coaching: Plan how ticket-level certified indicator acceptance coverage will be verified in-session — restate any deferred or unobservable success target as an observable proxy.
coaching.subject: ticket-level certified indicator acceptance coverage

coaching.coaching: Pin down pass-4 coaching match protocol for finding rows with a concrete worked example.
coaching.subject: pass-4 coaching match protocol for finding rows

coaching.coaching: Pin down pass-2 verification sub-answer keys and labels with a concrete worked example.
coaching.subject: pass-2 verification sub-answer keys and labels

coaching.coaching: Weigh at least one structural alternative for code-review gate verdict derivation from sidecar list.
coaching.subject: code-review gate verdict derivation from sidecar list

coaching.coaching: Record an ADR for round-index numbering convention for query param — it reads like a one-way door.
coaching.subject: round-index numbering convention for query param

coaching.coaching: Plan how line-graph oracle minimum non-empty series constraint will be verified in-session — restate any deferred or unobservable success target as an observable proxy.
coaching.subject: line-graph oracle minimum non-empty series constraint

coaching.coaching: Test the riskiest assumption behind convergence viz boundary at exactly three rounds first.
coaching.subject: convergence viz boundary at exactly three rounds

coaching.coaching: Plan how visual system contrast ratio done-definition will be verified in-session — restate any deferred or unobservable success target as an observable proxy.
coaching.subject: visual system contrast ratio done-definition

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC for the ticket-level certified indicator: assert that a ticket with a valid `attestations`/`signature` renders exactly one badge in the header area, and that a ticket with no attestation renders no badge — verifying both presence and the 'shown ONCE, never per-round' constraint.
evidence: 'A single ticket-level certified indicator (a quiet badge near the header) is shown when trail["ticket"] has a valid plan-review attestation — sourced from the ticket's attestations/signature, shown ONCE, never per-round.'
ABSENCE: no AC in the list verifies the badge's presence, its source field (attestations/signature), or the 'shown ONCE, never per-round' constraint.
findings.finding: The ticket-level certified indicator — 'a quiet badge near the header shown when trail["ticket"] has a valid plan-review attestation' — is a named, user-facing feature specified in both the 'What' section and the 'Trust signals' section, but no acceptance criterion exercises or verifies it. An agent implementing only what the ACs prove could omit it entirely and still pass all tests.
findings.location: What section — 'Ticket header (compact, collapsible)' bullet; AC list (no matching AC)
scenarios: Agent implements all ACs, skips the badge, CI is green — the feature is silently absent in the shipped page.
Agent renders the badge per-round (violating the 'shown ONCE' constraint) and no test catches the regression.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC: 'Oracle: an E2E test on a ticket whose `trail["ticket"]` carries a valid `attestations`/`signature` asserts (a) exactly one `.certified-badge` element appears in the `<header>` region, and (b) a ticket without attestations renders zero such elements. A separate assertion on a multi-round ticket confirms the badge count remains 1 regardless of round count.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Tighten AC3's oracle to name at least one specific field per pass that must appear in the rendered HTML (e.g. pass-1: `evidence` text; pass-2: a `verification` sub-answer; pass-3: `reason` text; pass-4: `move_name`), so a stub-heading implementation does not satisfy the criterion.
evidence: 'Oracle: an E2E test asserting the expanded finding HTML contains content from all four passes.'
ABSENCE: no named field values (e.g. `finding.evidence`, `finding.verification`, `finding.reason`) are required to be present in the assertion, only 'content from all four passes'.
findings.finding: AC3's oracle — 'asserting the expanded finding HTML contains content from all four passes' — does not specify what 'content' means (specific field values vs. presence of the four section headings). A test asserting only that the strings 'Finding', 'Verification', 'Decision', 'Coaching' appear in the DOM would pass without any actual field data being rendered, satisfying the letter of the AC while missing its intent.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC3
scenarios: An agent renders stub headings with no data and the AC passes.
A template bug that drops `pass-1 evidence` or `pass-2 verification` sub-answers goes undetected.
findings.suggested_fix: Revise AC3 oracle: '…asserting the expanded finding HTML contains (a) the finding's `evidence` string (pass-1), (b) at least one `verification` sub-answer string (pass-2), (c) the `reason` string (pass-3), and (d) the matched `move_name` string (pass-4).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify whether `acceptance_criteria` is actually a field emitted by the reducer into TicketState (it is absent from both `types.py:TicketState` and `ticket_state.schema.json`), and either add it to the read-layer contract if it exists in reducer output, or remove the claim that `trail['ticket']` exposes acceptance criteria and specify an alternate data source.
evidence: src/rebar/types.py:85-115 — TicketState TypedDict has no `acceptance_criteria` field; fields are title, status, assignee, description, attestations, etc.

src/rebar/schemas/ticket_state.schema.json — `properties` block lists no `acceptance_criteria`; `additionalProperties: true` means it could appear if the reducer emits it, but there is no documented field.
Plan quote: 'trail["ticket"] = rebar.show_ticket(...) — title, status, assignee, description, acceptance criteria, and the ticket-level attestations / signature'
findings.finding: The plan asserts `trail['ticket'] = rebar.show_ticket(...)` exposes 'acceptance criteria', and that the ticket header will render 'description + full acceptance criteria behind a details toggle'. The `TicketState` TypedDict (src/rebar/types.py:85-115) and `ticket_state.schema.json` have no `acceptance_criteria` field — the schema's `required` and `properties` sections confirm its absence. The read-layer contract does not expose this field, so the UI component rendering it would receive nothing.
findings.location: Dependencies section: 'trail["ticket"] = rebar.show_ticket(...) — title, status, assignee, description, acceptance criteria...' and Ticket header bullet: 'description + full acceptance criteria behind a details toggle'
scenarios: The ticket header template block for 'acceptance criteria' renders nothing or an empty section because the field is absent from the TicketState contract.
The AC oracle test (AC-3: finding row expands to four-pass provenance) is unaffected, but the ticket header acceptance-criteria toggle is silently broken for every ticket.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a code-review v1 fallback analogous to the plan-review v1 fallback: when a code-review sidecar's schema is `code_review_result_v1` (no `dropped`/`indeterminate` buckets), the four-series grouping treats those buckets as empty and optionally renders a quiet caption.
evidence: src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py:31: `ACCEPTED_SCHEMAS = ('code_review_result_v1', 'code_review_result_v2')`
src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py:286-327: `all_review_results` returns any schema in ACCEPTED_SCHEMAS
Plan asserts 'each sidecar is code_review_result_v2' without caveat about v1 records that may still be in the store
findings.finding: The plan states `trail['code_reviews']` has each sidecar as a `code_review_result_v2` payload, but the code-review `all_review_results` reader (src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py:286-327) accepts BOTH `code_review_result_v1` and `code_review_result_v2` (via `ACCEPTED_SCHEMAS`). The `code_review_result_v1` schema lacks the `dropped` and `indeterminate` buckets the plan's four-series mapping relies on. The plan provides no v1 fallback path for code-review rendering (unlike the plan-review v1 fallback for the threshold meter).
findings.location: Dependencies section: 'each sidecar is `code_review_result_v2` with SEPARATE buckets `blocking/advisory/dropped/indeterminate` + `coaching`'
scenarios: A ticket with an older code-review run returns a v1 sidecar that has no `dropped` or `indeterminate` buckets; the four-series grouping code tries to access these keys and either crashes or silently shows zero counts in those series.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify exactly which data field determines the 'operator-attested criterion lacking attestation' flag: clarify whether it is `citation == null` on the per-criterion `criteria[]` entry (from `completion_verifier_pass_v1`), or the absence of a ticket-level attestation (`trail['completion']['attestation'] is None`), or both conditions ANDed together.
evidence: Plan: 'An `operator-attested` criterion lacking a recorded attestation is explicitly flagged'
src/rebar/llm/contracts.py lines 102-117: Criterion has {criterion, met, citation: Citation|None, kind}; citation is per-criterion
src/rebar/audit/read.py lines 46-66: `_completion_attestation()` reads the ticket-level completion-verifier attestation (a signed op-cert), a separate signal from the per-criterion citation
The two signals measure different things: per-criterion citation = evidence the verifier cited; ticket-level attestation = a cryptographic signature that the verifier ran
findings.finding: The completion panel's 'flag an `operator-attested` criterion lacking a recorded attestation' mechanism is under-specified: the plan does not state which data path determines 'lacking a recorded attestation' — whether it is (a) the per-criterion `citation` field being `null` in the PASS `criteria[]` entry, or (b) `kind == 'operator-attested'` AND `trail['completion']['attestation']` is `None` at the ticket level. These two interpretations yield different behavior (e.g., a criterion can be `operator-attested` with a non-null `citation` but still have no ticket-level attestation signature, or vice versa).
findings.location: What section — Completion panel bullet; AC 7 — 'one operator-attested criterion with no recorded attestation'
scenarios: An `operator-attested` criterion with `citation=None` (no evidence cited) but a valid ticket-level attestation: interpretation (a) flags it, interpretation (b) does not.
An `operator-attested` criterion with a non-null citation but no ticket-level attestation signature: interpretation (b) flags it, interpretation (a) does not.
Implementer chooses the wrong path; the flag appears or disappears under conditions the auditor does not expect, undermining trust in the panel.
findings.suggested_fix: The most data-proximate signal is the per-criterion `citation` field: an `operator-attested` entry where `citation` is `null` means the verifier found no concrete evidence to cite. Specify: 'flag an `operator-attested` row where its `criteria[]` entry carries `citation == null`'. This is a single-record lookup and avoids a cross-record join.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan which Finding-slot fields (`evidence`, `scenarios`, `suggested_fix`) are present on code-review findings vs plan-review findings, and specify the fallback rendering for each absent field in the code-review context (e.g., 'omit silently' or 'show a quiet dash').
evidence: Plan: 'Finding (pass-1 `finding`, mapped `criteria`, `location`, `evidence`, `scenarios`, `impact`, `suggested_fix`)' — describes the provenance slot for all findings
src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py lines 415–416: `evidence` and `scenarios` are added in plan-review's `_slim()` enrichment — present only in plan-review sidecar
src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py lines 107–111: code-review findings are stored as-is from verdict buckets via `_with_norm_ids()` — no dedicated evidence/scenarios enrichment in build_payload
tests/unit/test_audit_code_review_v2.py: code-review finding fixtures carry `verification`, `block_threshold`, `decision` but not `evidence`/`scenarios` as explicit fields
findings.finding: The plan applies the four-pass finding-row provenance (Finding / Verification / Decision / Coaching) uniformly to 'Plan-review and code-review sections', but code-review findings in `code_review_result_v2` lack several fields that the provenance template reads for plan-review: specifically, code-review findings do NOT go through the plan-review `_slim()` lossless-v2 enrichment and may lack `evidence[]`, `scenarios[]`, and `suggested_fix` at the finding level (these are Pass-1 plan-review-specific fields). The plan never specifies what to render in the Finding pass-1 slot for a code-review finding row when these fields are absent.
findings.location: What section — 'The finding is the atomic unit'; Dependencies — 'Each finding carries `finding`, `criteria`, `location`, `evidence`, `scenarios`, `impact`, `suggested_fix`'
scenarios: Code-review finding row renders the 'Finding' pass-1 section — `evidence` and `scenarios` are absent from the finding dict, leaving the template to either crash, silently omit, or show placeholder text with no specified behavior.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC for the ticket-level certified indicator: one test on a ticket whose `trail['ticket']` has a valid attestation (asserting the badge renders with correct sourcing), and one on a ticket without (asserting the badge is absent).
evidence: 'A **single ticket-level certified indicator** (a quiet badge near the header) is shown when `trail["ticket"]` has a valid plan-review attestation — sourced from the ticket's `attestations`/`signature`, shown ONCE, never per-round.'
ABSENCE: no AC tests for presence/absence of the ticket-level certified badge or its sourcing logic
findings.finding: The ticket-level certified indicator (a quiet badge near the header shown when `trail['ticket']` has a valid plan-review attestation) is described as a deliverable in the 'What' section but has no covering acceptance criterion; no AC oracle verifies its presence, correct sourcing from `attestations`/`signature`, or — critically — its absence when attestation is absent.
findings.location: What section — 'Ticket header (compact, collapsible)'; Acceptance Criteria (all ACs)
scenarios: Implementer could omit the badge or wire it to the wrong field with no test catching the gap
Badge could mistakenly appear when attestation is absent
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'The ticket header renders a certified indicator badge when and only when `trail["ticket"]` carries a valid plan-review attestation (sourced from `attestations`/`signature`); the badge is absent when attestation is absent. Oracle: E2E test on one attested and one un-attested ticket.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the plan how the Coaching slot in each finding row is populated: state that a coaching note is matched to a finding when the finding's `id` appears in the coaching note's `finding_refs[]` list, and that the Coaching slot is omitted (not shown) when no note matches.
evidence: Plan: 'Coaching (pass-4 matched `coaching` move + note)' — no join algorithm stated
src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py lines 445–454: coaching[] carries `finding_refs` as the join key at the payload level, not on the finding itself
src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py lines 107–111: code-review sidecar stores coaching at verdict level with `finding_refs`, not embedded per-finding
tests/unit/test_audit_coaching_fidelity.py: confirms join is by `finding_refs` cross-referencing finding `id`
findings.finding: The plan specifies that finding-row provenance includes a 'Coaching (pass-4 matched coaching move + note)' slot for both plan-review and code-review sections, but never defines the match protocol — specifically that coaching notes are joined to findings via `finding_refs` (the coaching note's `finding_refs[]` list, matched against the finding's `id`). Without this join-key rule, a template implementer has no algorithm for populating the Coaching slot of a code-review (or plan-review) finding row.
findings.location: What section — 'The finding is the atomic unit' / finding-row provenance description; Approach rationale — 'passes are a pipeline artifact'
scenarios: Template renders a code-review finding row and must populate the Coaching slot — without knowing to join by `finding_refs` ∩ {finding.id}, the implementer either omits coaching or renders it incorrectly.
Plan-review finding row has the same gap: coaching is at payload level, not embedded on the finding dict; the template needs to know the join algorithm.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the 'The finding is the atomic unit' description: 'Coaching match: a coaching move is associated with a finding when the finding's `id` is present in the coaching note's `finding_refs[]`; if no coaching note matches, the Coaching slot is omitted from the provenance block.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the Verification sub-section display schema: enumerate which `binary` sub-answer keys are shown (all 7 graded sub-questions + the 2 conditional ones, or a curated subset), specify the yes/no/insufficient/na rendering (e.g., glyph or label), and note how plan-review vs code-review `severity_attributes` differ so the template handles both shapes.
evidence: Plan: 'Verification (pass-2 `verification` sub-answers + severity attributes)' — no key enumeration or label mapping

src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/verify.py lines 91–133: 9 binary sub-question keys (`is_verifiable`, `evidence_entails_finding`, `path_reachable`, `impact_follows_necessarily`, `no_viable_alternative_explanation`, `no_existing_mitigation`, `severity_claim_justified`, `committed_work_relies_on_unbacked_claim`, `respects_artifact_altitude`) with distinct na-default semantics for the last two

src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/verify.py lines 228–302 and 305–391: `plan_review_verification_model` and `code_review_verification_model` have distinct `severity_attributes` shapes (PlanSeverityAttrs vs CodeSeverityAttrs)
ABSENCE: no display schema or label mapping for binary sub-answers anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The plan's finding-row provenance lists 'Verification (pass-2 `verification` sub-answers + severity attributes)' but never specifies which sub-answer keys to surface, their human-readable labels, how to render the four-value vocabulary (yes/no/insufficient/na), or how the two different severity-attributes shapes (plan-review `PlanSeverityAttrs` vs code-review `CodeSeverityAttrs`) differ in the template. The actual `verification` dict has a `binary` object with 9 named sub-question keys (7 graded + 2 veto/conditional) and a separate `severity_attributes` object whose field set differs between plan-review and code-review. An implementer building the Jinja2 template cannot construct the Verification section without guessing this schema.
findings.location: What section — 'The finding is the atomic unit' / finding-row provenance; Scope — 'finding-provenance' partial
scenarios: Template author must render 9 binary sub-answers with yes/no/insufficient/na values — with no guidance on which to show, which are graded vs veto, or their human labels, the implementation will be ad-hoc.
Plan-review and code-review findings have different severity_attributes shapes; without a spec, the template may hard-code plan-review field names and silently break for code-review.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the aggregation rule for the CODE gate-status chip: define how a single verdict (PASS / FAIL / 'not run') and blocking count are derived from `trail['code_reviews']` (e.g., 'latest sidecar across all code-review artifacts; FAIL if any blocking finding exists; not run if list is empty').
evidence: Plan: 'each chip is an in-page anchor... CODE ⦸ not run' and 'PLAN ✗ FAIL · 3 blocking / COMPLETION ✓ PASS · 8/8 met / CODE ⦸ not run'
src/rebar/audit/read.py lines 96–141: `code_reviews` is a list of `{ticket_id, sidecars: list[dict]}` — multiple artifacts, multiple sidecars each
ABSENCE: no aggregation rule for deriving a single CODE verdict from list of code-review sidecars is specified anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The gate-status strip must show a CODE gate verdict (PASS / FAIL / 'not run'), but `trail['code_reviews']` from the read layer is a LIST of code-review artifact entries (each with their own `sidecars[]`), not a single aggregated verdict. The plan never specifies the aggregation rule: how the server route should derive a single CODE gate verdict + blocking count from multiple code-review sidecar records. For example: does the strip show FAIL if any sidecar has blocking findings? Does it show the latest sidecar's verdict? What if sidecars across multiple code-review artifacts conflict?
findings.location: What section — 'Gate-status strip (sticky)'; Dependencies — `trail['code_reviews']` shape
scenarios: A ticket has two code-review artifact tickets (e.g., one for two PRs): the strip must show one CODE chip — without an aggregation rule the implementer chooses arbitrarily.
A ticket has one code-review artifact with multiple sidecar rounds: the 'not run' / PASS / FAIL determination rule is undefined.

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the round-numbering convention explicitly (e.g. 'n=1 is the oldest retained round; n=N is the latest') so both the selector label and the query-param mapping are unambiguous, and update AC7's oracle to assert the specific `?plan_round=` value used for the non-latest round.
evidence: 'Round navigation via a server-rendered ◂ round n of N ▸ selector … the selector items are anchor links to the SAME page with a query string ?<gate>_round=<n>'
'An out-of-range or absent param falls back to the latest round.'
ABSENCE: no statement whether n=1 is the oldest retained round or the latest.
findings.finding: The plan states the round selector lists rounds 'newest-first' and defaults to the latest, but does not specify whether `?plan_round=n` indexes rounds with n=1 meaning 'oldest' or n=1 meaning 'newest' (i.e. display order vs chronological order). An agent must infer the convention, and AC7's oracle only checks that an older round renders different content — it does not pin the numbering direction.
findings.location: What section — '◂ round n of N ▸ selector' bullet; Acceptance Criteria AC7
scenarios: Agent implements n=1 as 'newest', a second agent reviewing or extending the code assumes n=1 is 'oldest'; the selector and URL params diverge.
AC7 passes either way because it only checks 'distinct content' not the actual n-value of the older round.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the selector bullet: 'Rounds are numbered chronologically: round 1 = oldest retained, round N = latest. The default `?plan_round=N` (or absent param) renders the latest.' Update AC7: '…requesting `?plan_round=1` (oldest) renders findings distinct from the latest round's findings.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Constrain the 4-round fixture in AC6 to populate at least 2 non-empty decision series (K ≥ 2), making the multi-polyline rendering path actually exercised by the oracle.
evidence: 'a 4-round ticket whose fixture populates K non-empty series asserts <svg class="conv-line"> with exactly K <polyline> elements (one per series)'
ABSENCE: no minimum value for K is stated; K=1 is technically valid under the AC.
findings.finding: In AC6's line-graph oracle, K (the number of non-empty series) is fixture-determined at test-write time and is not constrained to be ≥2 in the plan. A fixture with only 1 non-empty series across 4 rounds satisfies the letter of the AC ('exactly K polylines where K=1') while failing to exercise the multi-series rendering logic that motivates the ≥4-round branch.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC6 — '4-round ticket whose fixture populates K non-empty series'
scenarios: Agent writes a 4-round fixture with only BLOCKING non-empty (K=1); the test passes but the multi-polyline rendering path is never exercised.
A bug in rendering the 2nd and 3rd polylines is invisible to CI.
findings.suggested_fix: Revise AC6: 'a 4-round ticket whose fixture populates at least 2 non-empty decision series (e.g. BLOCKING and ADVISORY each present in ≥1 round) asserts <svg class="conv-line"> with exactly K ≥ 2 <polyline> elements…'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the convergence visualization AC oracle to include a 3-round ticket fixture, asserting `<div class="conv-bars">` with exactly 3 `.conv-bar` rows and no `.conv-line` svg.
evidence: '≤3 rounds → `<div class="conv-bars">`... ≥4 rounds → `<svg class="conv-line">`'
AC #6: 'a 2-round ticket asserts `<div class="conv-bars">`... a 4-round ticket whose fixture populates K non-empty series asserts `<svg class="conv-line">`'
ABSENCE: no 3-round oracle test
findings.finding: The convergence visualization description defines ≤3 rounds → bars and ≥4 rounds → line graph, making the 3-round case the lower boundary of the bars form; but the AC oracle only exercises a 2-round ticket (bars) and a 4-round ticket (lines), leaving the exact boundary (3 rounds → bars, not lines) unverified by any test.
findings.location: What section — 'Per-round convergence visualization'; AC #6 (convergence visualization oracle)
scenarios: An off-by-one in the branching condition (e.g., `< 3` instead of `<= 3`) would pass both existing oracle tests but render a line graph for a 3-round ticket
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC #6: 'and a 3-round ticket asserts `<div class="conv-bars">` with exactly 3 `.conv-bar` rows and NO `.conv-line` svg (boundary verification of the ≤3 threshold).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a contrast done-definition to the visual system spec: state minimum contrast ratios for body text (≥4.5:1), UI component boundaries (≥3:1 per WCAG 1.4.11), and chip text-on-fill (≥4.5:1), and add a lint/visual check to the test suite or design tokens.
evidence: 'a strict 3-level type scale; saturated color reserved for verdict + decision-series chips only (everything else neutral grey)'
ABSENCE: no mention of contrast ratio requirements or thresholds anywhere in the plan
WCAG 1.4.3 (Contrast Minimum) and 1.4.11 (Non-text Contrast) are not cited
findings.finding: The plan specifies 'saturated color reserved for verdict + decision-series chips' and 'neutral grey' for all other elements, but provides no observable done-definition for contrast ratios (WCAG 1.4.3 AA: 4.5:1 for normal text, 3:1 for large text/UI components); inline CSS implementing these colors could easily fail contrast thresholds with no spec constraint preventing it.
findings.location: What section — 'Visual system'; Scope section — 'inline CSS'
scenarios: Neutral grey body text on a white or near-white background may fall below 4.5:1
Decision-series chips with saturated fill colors may have insufficient contrast between chip text and chip background

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a focus-visibility constraint to the visual system spec: explicitly require `:focus-visible` styles for all interactive elements (`<summary>`, anchor chips, round selector anchors) and verify they meet WCAG 2.4.7 — do not suppress browser default outlines without a replacement.
evidence: 'one consistent chevron affordance' — implies CSS styling of `<summary>` elements
'Each chip is an in-page anchor' — implies styled `<a>` elements
ABSENCE: no mention of `:focus-visible` preservation or focus indicator specification
WCAG 2.4.7 not cited
findings.finding: The plan relies on native `<details>/<summary>` for keyboard support, but the inline CSS may reset or override browser default focus ring styles on `<summary>` elements and anchor chips (gate-strip chips, round selector anchors); WCAG 2.4.7 (Focus Visible) requires a visible focus indicator and WCAG 2.4.11 (Focus Appearance, AA in 2.2) requires adequate focus indicator size — neither is addressed in the plan's accessibility or visual system sections.
findings.location: What section — 'Visual system'; Approach rationale — 'native no-JS `<details>` + jump-linked anchors'
scenarios: Inline CSS that resets `outline: none` on `<summary>` or anchor elements (common in design-reset patterns) would fail WCAG 2.4.7
Keyboard-only auditors navigating the round selector or gate-strip chips would have no visible focus cue
findings.suggested_fix: Add to 'Visual system': 'All interactive elements (`<summary>` chevrons, gate-strip anchor chips, round-selector anchors) preserve or replace browser `:focus-visible` ring with a minimum 2px outline meeting WCAG 2.4.7; `outline: none` is prohibited without a visible replacement.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or CLAUDE.md section capturing the finding-centric IA decision (vs. pass-as-navigation), the neutral threshold meter rationale, and the convergence viz form-switch threshold, so the design intent survives story closure.
evidence: 'Finding-centric IA (finding = row; four passes = its in-place provenance) was chosen over pass-as-navigation after HCD critique'
'The neutral threshold meter replaces a signed green/red delta...'
No mention of ADR, CLAUDE.md, or design-doc update anywhere in Scope or Approach rationale
The plan notes this design is 'the product of two rounds of HCD critique' — i.e., a deliberate, non-obvious choice
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural decision — finding-centric IA (finding as atomic unit with four-pass provenance co-located in-place), the neutral threshold meter design, and the convergence visualization — but does not call for capturing this in an ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design doc. The rationale exists only in the plan/story, which won't be discoverable once the story is closed.
findings.location: Approach rationale section; Scope section (`src/rebar/audit/templates/`)
scenarios: A future developer modifying the audit page encounters the four-pass provenance layout and convergence viz, has no documented rationale for why findings (not passes) are the IA unit, and re-introduces a pass-centric refactor
A future developer sees the ≤3/≥4 branch and doesn't know the threshold's origin or whether it's tunable
findings.suggested_fix: Create `docs/adr/NNN-audit-page-ia.md` (or append to CLAUDE.md) covering: (1) why findings are the IA unit (auditor mental model > pipeline artifact), (2) why the threshold meter inherits decision-series color rather than green/red, and (3) the ≤3/≥4 round heuristic for bars-vs-line and how to adjust it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extract the convergence visualization form-switch threshold (currently ≤3/≥4 rounds) into a named constant (e.g., `CONV_BAR_MAX_ROUNDS = 3`) in `server.py` so it is tunable in one place and self-documenting.
evidence: '≤3 rounds → `<div class="conv-bars">`... ≥4 rounds → `<svg class="conv-line">`'
No mention of a named constant, config value, or environment override for this threshold
The plan acknowledges this is an adaptive design choice, not a fixed requirement
findings.finding: The convergence visualization's form-switch threshold (≤3 rounds → bar chart; ≥4 rounds → line chart) is hardcoded as a magic number in the rendering logic with no configuration point or named constant, even though the appropriate cutoff may need tuning as ticket review volumes grow.
findings.location: What section — 'Per-round convergence visualization adapts to round count'; Scope — `src/rebar/audit/templates/*.html` / `src/rebar/audit/server.py`
scenarios: After deployment, stakeholders decide bars look better up to 5 rounds; a developer must grep for the magic number across templates and server code
The threshold is duplicated between server.py (for rendering dispatch) and the E2E test fixture (which seeds exactly 2 and 4 rounds) — a change must be made in multiple places
findings.suggested_fix: In `server.py`: `CONV_BAR_MAX_ROUNDS = 3`; pass it to the template context so templates reference `conv_bar_max_rounds` rather than a literal `3`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a route-level error contract to the `/ticket/<id>` handler: specify a 404 response for unknown ticket IDs, a graceful 500 page (not a raw traceback) for unexpected `audit_trail()` exceptions, and at minimum a logged error line for each failure case so operators can diagnose issues.
evidence: Plan specifies empty/partial states only for absent gate data ('not yet run' panels), not for route-level errors such as ticket not found or `audit_trail()` raising an exception.
ABSENCE: no 404 handling, no exception handler, no logging/metric for the new route is mentioned anywhere in the plan.
'Additive on top of the server-scaffolding story's gated surface' — but no error contract for the new route is stated.
findings.finding: The `/ticket/<id>` route has no specified error-handling path for failures from `audit_trail()` — including a missing/unknown ticket ID (404), a malformed payload, or an unexpected exception — leaving the failure mode unspecified and likely resulting in an unhandled 500 rather than a meaningful error page. There is also no mention of logging or observability for the new route's failure cases.
findings.location: Scope: `src/rebar/audit/server.py` — `/ticket/<id>` route; Compatibility & rollback section
scenarios: An auditor navigates to `/ticket/<nonexistent-id>` and receives an unhandled stack trace rather than a clear 404 page.
A malformed `audit_trail()` payload (e.g., missing required key) causes an unhandled exception mid-render, producing a broken partial page with no diagnostic for the operator.
findings.suggested_fix: In `server.py`, wrap the `audit_trail()` call in a try/except: raise an HTTP 404 with a rendered 'Ticket not found' page when the ticket ID is unknown, and catch all other exceptions to return a rendered 500 error page (no raw traceback) while logging the exception at ERROR level. Add one log.info line on successful render for baseline observability.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a specified error path for the `/ticket/<id>` route: define what the server renders when `audit_trail` returns no record (404 page with a real `h2` heading) and when the read layer raises an exception (5xx page), and add an AC/oracle asserting the 404 case.
evidence: No AC or prose mentions what the route renders when `audit_trail(ticket_id)` returns no record or raises.
`rebar.audit.read.audit_trail(ticket_id) -> dict` — the plan treats this as always succeeding.
All E2E oracle descriptions seed valid tickets; no fixture for an invalid/missing ticket_id is specified.
findings.finding: The `/ticket/<id>` route has no specified error path for an invalid or non-existent ticket_id (404) or for a read-layer exception — an auditor hitting a bad URL or a transient read failure sees whatever the framework's default error page produces rather than a graceful, branded error state.
findings.location: Scope — `src/rebar/audit/server.py` route; Acceptance Criteria (all E2E tests assume valid ticket IDs)
scenarios: An auditor copies a wrong ticket ID into the URL — sees a raw 500 or framework traceback instead of a helpful message.
A ticket is deleted or the read layer is temporarily unavailable — same unhandled failure mode.
A CI/CD system navigates to a stale ticket link after the ticket is archived.
findings.suggested_fix: In `server.py`, wrap the `audit_trail` call and return a minimal error template (real `h3` heading, human-readable message) on `KeyError`/`None` (→ 404) and on unexpected exceptions (→ 500). Add one AC: 'Navigating to `/ticket/<nonexistent-id>` renders a 404 page with a real heading (not a traceback). Oracle: E2E test asserting HTTP 404 status and a heading element in the response body.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether Jinja2 is already declared in a scaffolding-story extra (or will be), or replace the Jinja2 template approach with the existing stdlib string-rendering pattern already used in `editor_server.py`; if Jinja2 is retained, add it to a declared optional extra in `pyproject.toml` and cite the rule-of-three justification.
evidence: pyproject.toml lists only pyyaml, jsonschema, referencing as core deps; Jinja2 appears in none of the extras (mcp, reviewbot, agents, eval, tracing, grounding, dev).
grep for 'import jinja2' / 'from jinja2' / 'Jinja2Templates' across src/rebar returns zero hits.
src/rebar/llm/workflow/editor_server.py uses stdlib http.server + a Python-string render_host_html callback to serve HTML from a loopback server — the existing pattern for this exact use-case.
Rule-of-Three: 0 existing Jinja2 call-sites in src/rebar.
findings.finding: The plan introduces Jinja2 as the templating engine for `src/rebar/audit/templates/*.html` but Jinja2 is not declared in `pyproject.toml` under any dependency or optional extra, and has zero existing call-sites in `src/rebar/`. The codebase already uses a working stdlib HTML-rendering pattern (Python-string `render_host_html` callback in `editor_server.py`) for the same use-case (loopback server-rendered HTML). Adding Jinja2 is a net-new third-party dependency with no rule-of-three call-site justification and a working in-codebase alternative.
findings.location: Scope section — 'New src/rebar/audit/templates/*.html (Jinja2)'
scenarios: The audit server extra would silently break on a plain `pip install nava-rebar` without Jinja2, unless it is added to a declared extra — but that extra is never defined in the plan.
If the scaffolding story uses stdlib http.server (matching the editor_server.py pattern), then Jinja2 would be a dependency added for a single file set with no prior codebase use.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief interface contract to the Scope section specifying what this story inherits from the depends_on story's server.py — at minimum: the FastAPI app instance name/import path, whether Jinja2Templates is configured by a3d7 or by this story, and the template directory convention (`src/rebar/audit/templates/`).
evidence: Plan Scope: 'src/rebar/audit/server.py — add a /ticket/<id> route consuming rebar.audit.read.audit_trail, plus a tiny per-gate round selector reading ?<gate>_round=<n> and the four-series mapping helper.'

Depends-on story a3d7 Scope: 'New src/rebar/audit/server.py (< 500 LOC) — the FastAPI app + a serve(host, port) entry, lazy-imported inside the CLI handler... one index route enumerating audited tickets'
a3d7 file_impact does NOT include any Jinja2Templates setup or templates/ directory
src/rebar/audit/server.py is currently absent (confirmed by directory listing and ticket attestation 'dep absent src/rebar/audit/server.py')
findings.finding: The plan names src/rebar/audit/server.py as an edit target for this story ('add a /ticket/<id> route consuming rebar.audit.read.audit_trail'), but server.py is also the primary created artifact of the depends_on story (a3d7), which adds the FastAPI app, the serve entry point, and one index route. The plan gives no interface contract for what a3d7 will leave in server.py — the FastAPI app instance name, whether Jinja2Templates is already configured there, and the template directory convention are all unspecified. An implementer of this story cannot know what to import from or append to without that contract.
findings.location: Scope section: 'src/rebar/audit/server.py — add a /ticket/<id> route'
scenarios: The a3d7 implementer ships server.py without Jinja2Templates (using plain HTMLResponse for the index); this story's implementer then needs to add Jinja2Templates from scratch while also extending the app — the integration point is invisible.
The a3d7 implementer names the FastAPI app instance 'app' vs 'audit_app'; this story adds routes to a symbol it cannot know in advance.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope section: 'This story extends the FastAPI `app` instance created by the server-scaffolding story in `server.py`. The Jinja2Templates instance (pointing at `src/rebar/audit/templates/`) is initialized in this story (the scaffolding story serves its index page via a plain template or HTMLResponse and does not set up the templates directory); the template directory `src/rebar/audit/templates/` is also created by this story.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare `jinja2` (or `fastapi[jinja2]`) as an explicit dependency in the plan's new audit-server extra (or an existing appropriate extra such as `reviewbot`), so the template rendering mechanism is installable without a dev venv.
evidence: Plan: 'New `src/rebar/audit/templates/*.html` (Jinja2)'
pyproject.toml search: no `jinja2` entry in any extra (reviewbot = ["fastapi", "uvicorn[standard]", "boto3"]; agents, eval, tracing, grounding have no jinja2)
src/rebar/review_bot/app.py and src/rebar/opcert_service/app.py use `from fastapi.responses import JSONResponse` — never Jinja2Templates
resolve_symbol confirms jinja2 is installed in the dev venv but this does not mean it is declared as a package dependency
findings.finding: The plan mandates Jinja2 file-based templates (`src/rebar/audit/templates/*.html`) but `jinja2` is not declared in any `pyproject.toml` optional-dependency extra, and no existing audit server code (reviewbot, opcert_service) uses Jinja2 — both use FastAPI + JSONResponse only. Running the route will raise ImportError unless a new `[ui]` (or equivalent) extra is declared and `jinja2` is added to it.
findings.location: Scope section — `src/rebar/audit/templates/*.html` (Jinja2); `pyproject.toml` optional-dependencies
scenarios: A user installs `nava-rebar[reviewbot]` and starts `rebar audit serve`; the `/ticket/<id>` route crashes with ImportError on `from fastapi.templating import Jinja2Templates` because jinja2 is not a transitive of fastapi alone.
`make test` passes in the dev venv (jinja2 is installed) but CI on a fresh install without jinja2 fails at the template-rendering step.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `jinja2>=3` to the `reviewbot` extra in `pyproject.toml` (since the audit server shares the FastAPI/uvicorn stack already in that extra), OR define a new `[ui]` extra and document it in the Scope section. Update the plan's Scope or Compatibility section to name the extra.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-sentence rationale in the Approach rationale section confirming that server-side SVG/chart libraries compatible with the no-external-JS constraint were considered and rejected (e.g., too heavy a dependency for a `<polyline>`-only need) so the custom inline SVG choice satisfies the anti-repackaging criterion.
evidence: 'the convergence visualization + threshold meter as inline SVG/CSS (no external JS/asset)' — no mention of alternatives within the same constraint
The plan invokes the no-JS constraint to rule out Chart.js/D3, but does not mention server-side SVG libraries that would be compatible with the constraint
findings.finding: The plan asserts that inline `<svg>`/`<polyline>` is sufficient for the convergence visualization without acknowledging whether server-side SVG generation libraries (e.g., Matplotlib SVG export, CairoSVG, or pure-CSS chart alternatives) were considered within the no-external-JS constraint. For a dual-mode visualization (≤3 rounds → div bars; ≥4 rounds → SVG polyline) hand-rolled in a Jinja2 template, there is real prior art the plan should at minimum acknowledge rejecting.
findings.location: Scope section / 'New src/rebar/audit/templates/*.html … the convergence visualization … as inline SVG/CSS (no external JS/asset)'
scenarios: A future maintainer adds rounds and discovers the hand-rolled polyline chart has viewBox scaling or label-overlap bugs that a library would have handled, with no record of why the library was rejected
The dual-mode threshold (≤3 div-bars vs ≥4 SVG) adds branching complexity that a minimal server-side helper library could eliminate
findings.suggested_fix: Add to 'Approach rationale': 'Server-side SVG libraries (e.g., Matplotlib SVG, svgwrite) were considered and rejected: the visualization requires only `<polyline>` elements with static viewBox coordinates, making a full library dependency disproportionate to the need; the no-external-asset constraint rules out client-side charting libraries.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly declare `jinja2` (or use `"fastapi[jinja2]"`) in the new `[ui]` pyproject.toml extra that this story's server-scaffolding dependency is expected to define, since FastAPI does not bundle jinja2 and the current `reviewbot` extra omits it.
evidence: Plan: 'New src/rebar/audit/templates/*.html (Jinja2)'
pyproject.toml `reviewbot` extra: `["fastapi", "uvicorn[standard]", "boto3"]` — jinja2 absent
grep of pyproject.toml for 'jinja2': zero matches across all extras and [project.dependencies]
`fastapi.templating.Jinja2Templates` EXISTS in the dev venv (verified) — but ONLY because jinja2 is incidentally installed; the pyproject.toml declared state does not guarantee it
FastAPI docs: jinja2 is an optional dep — `pip install 'fastapi[jinja2]'` is the supported path; bare `fastapi` omits it
Evidence class: PARTIALLY-VERIFIED — the surface works when jinja2 is present, but the plan's install path does not declare it
findings.finding: The plan's Jinja2 server-rendering integration depends on `jinja2` being installed, but `jinja2` is declared nowhere in `pyproject.toml` — not in `[project.dependencies]`, not in the `reviewbot` extra (`["fastapi", "uvicorn[standard]", "boto3"]`), and not in any other extra. FastAPI explicitly treats jinja2 as an OPTIONAL dep (`fastapi[jinja2]`); a bare `pip install fastapi` does NOT pull it. The current venv has jinja2 available only as an incidental transitive, not as a guaranteed declared dep. The plan names no `[ui]` extra definition and lists no pip deps for it, so the new Jinja2Templates render path has an undeclared precondition that will cause `ImportError` on a clean install.
findings.location: Scope section: `src/rebar/audit/templates/*.html (Jinja2)`; Approach rationale: 'server-rendered from the read layer over native no-JS <details>'; Compatibility & rollback: 'Additive on top of the server-scaffolding story's gated surface'
scenarios: A user or CI job installs `nava-rebar[ui]` (or whatever extra the server-scaffolding story defines) without jinja2 transitively present — `Jinja2Templates(directory=...)` raises `ImportError: 'jinja2' package is not installed` at server startup, breaking all AC E2E tests.
A future FastAPI release that changes its optional-dep resolution could silently drop the incidental transitive, breaking existing installs without any code change in rebar.
findings.suggested_fix: In `pyproject.toml`, add `"jinja2>=3"` (or `"fastapi[jinja2]"`) to the new `[ui]` optional-dependency group. If the server-scaffolding story owns that extra definition, note the dep requirement explicitly in this plan so the scaffolding story's author includes it: e.g., `ui = ["fastapi", "uvicorn[standard]", "jinja2>=3"]`.

findings.finding: The plan asserts 'timestamp' as an available trust signal in each plan-review round header, but the `all_review_results()` function (consumed by `audit_trail`) extracts only `event['data']` from each sidecar file, discarding the outer event envelope's `timestamp` field. The sidecar payload (`plan_review_result_v2`) has no timestamp field of its own. The `AuditTrail` TypedDict exposes no timestamp per round. The server cannot display per-round timestamps as claimed without additional work not described in the plan.
findings.location: What / Trust signals subsection; also Scope `src/rebar/audit/server.py`
findings.suggested_fix: In `all_review_results()`, return `{'ts': <filename_prefix_ns>, **payload}` (or an equivalent wrapper dict) so `audit_trail`'s `plan_reviews` list includes the nanosecond timestamp from the filename, which the server can format for display.

findings.finding: The plan asserts 'a distinct certified badge near the verdict when the record is signed' in each plan-review round header. Plan-review signing produces a ticket-level attestation (`SIGNATURE` event via `attest.py`), not a per-round field in the sidecar. The `AuditTrail` read layer (`read.py`) exposes no plan-review attestation status — only the completion attestation is read (`_completion_attestation`). Displaying a per-round certified badge would require an additional `verify_signature(ticket_id, kind=_MANIFEST_PREFIX)` call the plan never specifies.
findings.location: What / Trust signals subsection

findings.finding: The plan promises a `timestamp` trust signal in each round header ('gate/model version, timestamp'), but both `plan_review.sidecar.all_review_results` and `code_review.sidecar.all_review_results` return only the inner `event['data']` payload dict — which does NOT include `timestamp`. The outer event envelope (stripped by both readers) carries the timestamp; the inner payload does not. The scope lists only `server.py` and templates as edit targets, with no mention of extending the read layer to surface per-round timestamps.
findings.location: Scope section (`src/rebar/audit/server.py`); plan 'Trust signals' bullet; `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py` lines 205–215 (`all_review_results`); `src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py` lines 310–319 (`all_review_results`)
findings.suggested_fix: Extend `audit/read.py` (or both sidecar `all_review_results` helpers) to inject the outer envelope `timestamp` into each returned payload dict (e.g. `payload['_ts'] = event.get('timestamp')`), then add `audit/read.py` to the Scope edit list and update the AC trust-signals oracle to assert the timestamp field is present.

findings.finding: The completion panel's per-criterion row mechanism breaks silently when only a FAIL completion sidecar exists. The plan states the panel reads story C's `criteria[]` field (met/not-met/kind/citation), but the `completion_verifier_fail_v1` schema (the FAIL sidecar) does NOT carry `criteria[]` — only `findings[]` (failures only). The `audit_trail` read layer returns the PASS sidecar when it exists (which carries `criteria[]`), but when only a FAIL record exists, `criteria[]` is absent. The template receives no per-criterion rows to render for a ticket that has failed completion but never passed.
findings.location: What section — Completion section; src/rebar/llm/completion_sidecar.py build_payload (FAIL branch, lines 222–231)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a fallback rendering path: when `completion.sidecar.schema == 'completion_verifier_fail_v1'` (or `criteria[]` is absent), display the failure findings from `findings[]` in the criterion rows instead, with a 'not-met' glyph and a note that positive criteria detail requires a PASS record. Update the relevant AC oracle to cover this case.

findings.finding: The convergence-viz AC for a ≥4-round ticket asserts 'exactly 4 `<polyline>`' but the plan defines polyline count as 'one `<polyline>` per decision series' (BLOCKING, advisory, dropped, indeterminate), not per round. A 4-round ticket whose findings only hit two decision series (e.g., BLOCKING and advisory) would produce 2 polylines, making the assertion falsely fail. Conversely, a 5-round ticket with 4 decision series also satisfies 'exactly 4' — the count is not tied to rounds at all.
findings.location: AC for convergence visualization: 'a 4-round ticket asserts an `<svg class="conv-line">` with exactly 4 `<polyline>` (one per decision series) and NO `.conv-bars`'
findings.suggested_fix: Rewrite the oracle as: 'a 4-round ticket whose fixture data contains exactly K distinct decision series asserts an `<svg class="conv-line">` with exactly K `<polyline>` elements and NO `.conv-bars`; and asserts the visually-hidden `<table>` has exactly 4 data rows (one per round).' This separates the round-count assertion (table rows) from the series-count assertion (polylines).

findings.finding: The convergence visualization AC oracle for ≥4-round tickets asserts 'exactly 4 `<polyline>` (one per decision series)', but the plan-review sidecar buckets findings into up to 5 series (blocking / advisory / overflow / indeterminate / dropped), making '4 polylines' an internally inconsistent oracle that will either be wrong for typical data or require the implementer to silently merge or drop a series.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC6; What section — convergence visualization (≥4 rounds, `<svg class="conv-line">`)
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section, add: 'For the line graph, the `overflow` series is merged into `advisory` (both are non-blocking surfaced findings); the four rendered series are: BLOCKING / ADVISORY (includes overflow) / DROPPED / INDETERMINATE.' Update the AC6 oracle to 'exactly 4 `<polyline>` representing blocking, advisory+overflow, dropped, and indeterminate series.'

findings.finding: The plan asserts 'the server reads [block_threshold] directly off the finding record' and the threshold meter AC requires each finding's tick position to reflect its own persisted threshold. However, v1 sidecar records (`plan_review_result_v1`) do not carry `block_threshold` on findings — confirmed by test `test_sidecar_reader.py:190` (`assert 'block_threshold' not in got_v1['findings'][0]`). `all_review_results()` returns both v1 and v2 payloads. The plan never specifies what the threshold meter should render when `block_threshold` is absent (older rounds or v1 history).
findings.location: What / Plan-review and code-review sections / threshold display; AC for threshold meter
findings.suggested_fix: In the template, treat a missing `block_threshold` as `DEFAULT_BLOCK_THRESHOLD = 0.95` (matching the historical default the criterion would have been judged against) and note this in the tick's tooltip.

findings.finding: The threshold meter and four-pass provenance display have no specified fallback for `plan_review_result_v1` sidecar records, which lack `block_threshold`, `evidence`, and `scenarios` fields (confirmed by the codebase: v1 records never carry these fields). The plan states the meter 'reads each finding's persisted `block_threshold`/`priority`/`decision`' and provenance displays 'pass-1 evidence', but does not specify what the template renders when these fields are absent on a v1 finding.
findings.location: What section — threshold meter; four-pass provenance; tests/interfaces/store/test_sidecar_reader.py lines 171–190
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope/templates section: 'Templates must guard all v2-only fields (`block_threshold`, `evidence`, `scenarios`) with `{% if finding.block_threshold is not none %}` / `{% if finding.evidence %}` guards; v1 findings render the meter bar (priority fill) without a tick, and the provenance block omits the evidence/scenarios subsections with no visible gap.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
